examples.title: FCFS Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First Come First Serve non-preemptive scheduling
examples.code:
def fcfs_scheduling(processes):
    # processes: list of tuples (pid, arrival_time, burst_time)
    processes.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # Sort by arrival time
    current_time = 0
    total_waiting_time = 0
    total_turnaround_time = 0
    
    for pid, at, bt in processes:
        if current_time < at:
            current_time = at
        waiting_time = current_time - at
        turnaround_time = waiting_time + bt
        current_time += bt
        
        total_waiting_time += waiting_time
        total_turnaround_time += turnaround_time
    
    n = len(processes)
    print(f"Average Waiting Time: {total_waiting_time/n}")
    print(f"Average Turnaround Time: {total_turnaround_time/n}")

# Example: [(pid, arrival_time, burst_time)]
processes = [(1, 0, 10), (2, 1, 5), (3, 2, 8)]
fcfs_scheduling(processes)
examples.explanation: This FCFS implementation sorts processes by arrival time and executes them in order. Each process waits from its arrival until CPU becomes free, then executes for its full burst time.

examples.title: SJF Non-Preemptive Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of Shortest Job First scheduling
examples.code:
def sjf_non_preemptive(processes):
    # processes: list of tuples (pid, arrival_time, burst_time)
    processes.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # Sort by arrival
    result = []
    current_time = 0
    completed = 0
    n = len(processes)
    
    while completed < n:
        # Find shortest job among arrived processes
        ready_processes = [p for p in processes 
                          if p[1] <= current_time and p not in result]
        
        if ready_processes:
            # Select shortest burst time
            shortest = min(ready_processes, key=lambda x: x[2])
            result.append(shortest)
            
            waiting_time = current_time - shortest[1]
            turnaround_time = waiting_time + shortest[2]
            current_time += shortest[2]
            completed += 1
        else:
            current_time += 1
    
    return result

processes = [(1, 0, 7), (2, 1, 4), (3, 2, 1), (4, 3, 4)]
execution_order = sjf_non_preemptive(processes)
examples.explanation: SJF selects the process with minimum burst time from all arrived processes. It minimizes average waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times in advance.

examples.title: Round Robin Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of Round Robin with time quantum
examples.code:
def round_robin(processes, quantum):
    # processes: list of tuples (pid, arrival_time, burst_time)
    from collections import deque
    
    # Sort by arrival and add to queue
    processes.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
    ready_queue = deque()
    
    # Track remaining burst times
    remaining = {p[0]: p[2] for p in processes}
    
    current_time = 0
    completed = []
    i = 0  # Index for new arrivals
    n = len(processes)
    
    ready_queue.append(processes[0])
    i = 1
    
    while ready_queue:
        pid, at, bt = ready_queue.popleft()
        
        # Execute for quantum or remaining time
        exec_time = min(quantum, remaining[pid])
        remaining[pid] -= exec_time
        current_time += exec_time
        
        # Add newly arrived processes to queue
        while i < n and processes[i][1] <= current_time:
            ready_queue.append(processes[i])
            i += 1
        
        # If not completed, add back to queue
        if remaining[pid] > 0:
            ready_queue.append((pid, at, bt))
        else:
            turnaround = current_time - at
            waiting = turnaround - bt
            completed.append((pid, waiting, turnaround))
    
    # Print results
    for pid, wt, tat in completed:
        print(f"P{pid}: WT={wt}, TAT={tat}")

processes = [(1, 0, 10), (2, 1, 5), (3, 2, 8)]
round_robin(processes, quantum=4)
examples.explanation: Round Robin uses a ready queue and time quantum. Each process executes for at most quantum time, then moves to queue end. Ensures fairness and good response time.

examples.title: Priority Scheduling (Preemptive)
examples.description: C implementation of preemptive priority scheduling
examples.explanation: This preemptive priority scheduling selects the highest priority ready process at each time unit. If a higher priority process arrives, it preempts the currently running process.
